metadata.districtModel: five sections; five district slots per section; D13 is the seat of power

cityDNA.coreFantasy: A Irish / Celtic city where Oath-Ring Hillfort of the Green Spiral, Covered Bazaar Beacon, Spiral Knotwork Sovereign Registry Court, and the five-section district spine turn local identity into functional civic space.
cityDNA.readerTakeaway: Dun Liath should feel immediately identifiable through exact landmarks and materials, not through generic fantasy-city shorthand.
cityDNA.civilizationThesis: Dun Liath organizes power through Spiral Knotwork Sovereign Registry Court, public response through Covered Bazaar Beacon, civic memory through Oath-Ring Hillfort of the Green Spiral, and daily life through S1 Grand Market Civic Hall, S2 Artisan Guilds Civic Hall, S3 Records / Archives Hall, S4 Low Residential Civic Hall, S5 Entertainment / Theater Civic Hall.
cityDNA.visualIdentity: Granite, Peat Brick, Oak, Copper, Dyed Wool, Dry Stone Paving, Civic Terraces, Heather Highland, Civic Blue Illumination, Oath Seals, Ancestral Spirits, and exact anchors including Spiral Knotwork Sovereign Registry Court, Covered Bazaar Beacon, Oath-Ring Hillfort of the Green Spiral, Opera Court, Guildhall, Tenement Court, Grand Market Civic Hall, Awning Monument, Auction Court Nook, Artisan Guilds Civic Hall.
cityDNA.culturalIdentity: A Irish / Celtic civic culture where architecture, public obligations, roads, landmark approaches, and everyday work must all read as part of the same civilization.
cityDNA.symbolicIdentity: The city symbol system is built from exact names and visible functions: Oath-Ring Hillfort of the Green Spiral, Covered Bazaar Beacon, Spiral Knotwork Sovereign Registry Court, and section anchors such as Grand Market Civic Hall, Artisan Guilds Civic Hall, Records / Archives Hall, Low Residential Civic Hall, Entertainment / Theater Civic Hall.

canonLocks: Dun Liath uses city code DNL.
The Wonder is Oath-Ring Hillfort of the Green Spiral.
The Beacon is Covered Bazaar Beacon.
The Seat of Power is Spiral Knotwork Sovereign Registry Court and occupies District 13.
The city uses five sections with five tactical district slots per section.
The source-backed section spine is: S1 Grand Market Civic Hall, S2 Artisan Guilds Civic Hall, S3 Records / Archives Hall, S4 Low Residential Civic Hall, S5 Entertainment / Theater Civic Hall.

rejectedReads: Do not treat Dun Liath as a generic fantasy settlement with renamed landmarks.
Do not collapse the five-section board into a single mood or single district.
Do not replace exact anchor names with generic summaries.
Do not invent missing street-section landmarks when a street source is absent.
cautionFlags: Keep every named Wonder, Beacon, Seat, section civic hall, major landmark, monument, and minor nook exact when present in sidecars.
If an image appears to deviate from a sidecar, preserve the exact name but avoid overclaiming visual details not supported by the source package.
Every district asset should remain usable for narrative scenes, tactical board dioramas, and city map backgrounds.

environment.environmentalLogic: The environment should make Dun Liath's exact civic anchors plausible at street scale: terrain routes people toward section landmarks, lighting clarifies public authority, and water or landscape features remain source-backed rather than decorative filler.

skyline: Oath-Ring Hillfort of the Green Spiral
infrastructure: records, permits, markets, services, or rituals implied by anchors
colorLanguage: local textile, stone, metal, vegetation, or water colors from the image package

civilization.people: The population should read as Irish / Celtic and city-specific: citizens occupy streets, shops, public thresholds, and landmark approaches as people who understand the local civic order.
socialClasses: laborers, carriers, messengers, and maintenance crews
households tied to local district obligations
civilization.governance: Government is centered on Spiral Knotwork Sovereign Registry Court. Its authority should be visible through courts, records, public access routes, civic lamps, guards or attendants, and the way citizens respond to D13.
civilization.religion: Religious or ritual life should only be emphasized where source anchors support it, especially names such as Oath-Ring Hillfort of the Green Spiral.

citizens orient public life around Artisan Guilds Civic Hall in Section 2

citizens orient public life around Entertainment / Theater Civic Hall in Section 5
beacon changes become public behavior, not background color

civic errands happen visibly at thresholds, queues, ledgers, stalls, courts, or shrines
bodyLanguage: workers move with practiced familiarity around district anchors
officials use thresholds and raised places to control attention
residents treat named landmarks as everyday orientation points
speechPatterns: public notices are tied to section anchors
local speech distinguishes civic authority, ordinary errands, and landmark obligations

streets.appearance: Streets should show Dun Liath's exact anchor system at pedestrian scale: S1 Grand Market Civic Hall, S2 Artisan Guilds Civic Hall, S3 Records / Archives Hall, S4 Low Residential Civic Hall, S5 Entertainment / Theater Civic Hall. Keep roads, plazas, stairs, thresholds, lamps, signs-without-readable-text, props, and civilians aligned to the source-backed materials and landmarks.
streets.movement: Movement should be readable for tactical-board assets: citizens pass between 25 district slots, section anchors, D13, service streets, and landmark approaches without visual clutter hiding the civic purpose.
sounds: landmark-specific sounds from water, bells, animals, machines, or crowds when source-backed
smells: worn stone, timber, metal, plaster, or fabric
streets.lighting: Use the package's source-backed civic lighting, daylight/golden-hour reads, lanterns, and beacon glow. Lighting must help identify authority, safety, and landmark function.
streets.weatherEffects: Weather should reinforce the local terrain and materials without overwhelming the city identity or inventing unsupported climate extremes.
streets.dayCycle: Day scenes emphasize errands, trade, records, repairs, rituals, food, and section-level movement.
streets.nightCycle: Night scenes emphasize lantern routes, closed thresholds, guarded landmarks, quiet work, and beacon visibility.

common: street services tied to section anchors
exports: specialized tools or ritual goods as supported by anchors
imports: dyes, oils, spices, timber, stone, metal, glass, or cloth as needed by the architecture

housing.wealthy: Prestige housing clusters near Spiral Knotwork Sovereign Registry Court, high terraces, or landmark-facing routes, using the best source-backed materials and controlled views of civic space.
housing.middle: Shop-over-house units, courtyard homes, guild-adjacent apartments, and family rooms near section anchors.
housing.poor: Smaller back-street rooms, work-adjacent housing, shared courtyards, service alleys, patched materials, and dense but still city-specific lived-in detail.
